perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT will maintain adequate SAFEGUARDS to PROTECT such CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL and PREVENT its DISCLOSURE to others, except its own employees on a NEED-TO-KNOW BASIS, and will NEITHER USE nor DISCLOSE, DIRECTLY or INDIRECTLY, for him/herself or for the benefit of another, ANY CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL or perform any acts which may DIRECTLY or INDIRECTLY have an adverse effect upon the business of the COMPANY, or which would tend to reduce the proprietary value of such CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL to the COMPANY, without the COMPANY'S PRIOR WRITTEN APPROVAL.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT will use reasonable efforts to PROTECT such CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL and PREVENT its DISCLOSURE to others, and may USE or DISCLOSE, DIRECTLY or INDIRECTLY, for him/herself or for the benefit of another, ANY CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL or perform any acts which may DIRECTLY or INDIRECTLY have an adverse effect upon the business of the COMPANY, with the COMPANY'S PRIOR WRITTEN APPROVAL.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'maintain adequate safeguards' to 'use reasonable efforts' and negating the restriction on using confidential material significantly weakens the protection of trade secrets. This creates an omission by contradicting Texas law protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: TUTSA defines misappropriation of trade secrets broadly, including unauthorized disclosure or use. The original text prevents this. The modified text allows disclosure or use with approval, but weakens the standard of care to 'reasonable efforts,' and creates the possiblity of disclosure without written approval. This contradicts TUTSA's goal of protecting trade secrets from misappropriation, as it provides a legal 'loophole' for a recipient to disclose or use the trade secret, especially because 'reasonable efforts' provides such a low bar to meet
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 134A. TRADE SECRETS CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 6. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS CHAPTER 134A. TRADE SECRETS Sec. 134A.001. SHORT TITLE. This chapter may be cited as the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Added by Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2013. Sec. 134A.002. DEFINITIONS. In this chapter: (1) "Claimant" means a party seeking to recover damages under this chapter, including a plaintiff, counterclaimant, cross-claimant, or third-party plaintiff. In an action in which a party seeks recovery of damages under this chapter on behalf of another person, "claimant" includes both that other person and the party seeking recovery of damages. (1-a) "Clear and convincing" means the measure or degree of proof that will produce in the mind of the trier of fact a firm belief or conviction as to the truth of the allegations sought to be established. (2) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, to limit use, or to prohibit discovery of a trade secret, or espionage through electronic or other means. (3) "Misappropriation" means: (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the person's knowledge of the trade secret was: (a) derived from or through a person who used improper means to acquire the trade secret; (b) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain the secrecy of or limit the use of the trade secret; or (c) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain the secrecy of or limit the use of the trade secret; or (iii) before a material change of the position of the person, knew or had reason to know that the trade secret was a trade secret and that knowledge of the trade secret had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3-a) "Owner" means, with respect to a trade secret, the person or entity in whom or in which rightful, legal, or equitable title to, or the
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a significant legal contradiction by weakening the protection of trade secrets, as defined by the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act (TUTSA). The original text mandated 'adequate SAFEGUARDS,' while the changed text only requires 'reasonable efforts.' Additionally, the original text prohibited unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential material, whereas the changed text permits such actions with the company's prior written approval. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, TUTSA defines 'Misappropriation' as 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' Although the modified text requires 'prior written approval,' the reduced standard of care to 'reasonable efforts' weakens the protection and increases the risk of misappropriation. This directly contradicts the intent of TUTSA to protect trade secrets from unauthorized disclosure or use.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT shall MAINTAIN a RECORD or LOG reflecting the NAMES, ADDRESSES, DATES and other INFORMATION which the COMPANY shall request RECIPIENT to keep of PERSONS or ENTITIES to execute a COPY of this AGREEMENT acknowledging its TERMS and the TRADE SECRET nature of CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL and shall provide to the COMPANY UPON REQUEST, copies of ALL such acknowledgments.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT may maintain a RECORD or LOG reflecting the NAMES, ADDRESSES, DATES and other INFORMATION of PERSONS or ENTITIES to execute a COPY of this AGREEMENT acknowledging its TERMS and the TRADE SECRET nature of CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL and may provide to the COMPANY copies of such acknowledgments.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain' to 'may maintain' and 'shall provide upon request' to 'may provide' removes the mandatory requirement for the recipient to keep records of individuals who have access to the confidential information and to provide those records to the company. This omission weakens the company's ability to track and control access to its trade secrets and creates an omission by contradicting Texas law protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the log and provision of records optional, the modified text undermines the 'reasonable efforts' a company must undertake to maintain secrecy, thus weakening their claim under TUTSA if misappropriation occurs. The original provision enables a company to know who has access to their trade secrets. Removing that ability makes it harder for the trade secret holder to enforce their rights.

TRADE SECRETS Sec. 134A.001. SHORT TITLE. This chapter may be cited as the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Added by Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2013. In an action in which a party seeks recovery of damages under this chapter on behalf of another person, "claimant" includes both that other person and the party seeking recovery of damages. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies 'shall maintain' to 'may maintain' and 'shall provide upon request' to 'may provide', thereby removing the mandatory nature of record-keeping and provision to the company. The law snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' which includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By making the record-keeping optional, the modified contract weakens the company's ability to maintain secrecy, potentially hindering its ability to enforce its rights under TUTSA if misappropriation occurs. The original contract ensures accountability by requiring records, whereas the modified version makes it discretionary. This directly undermines the 'reasonable efforts' a company should take to protect its trade secrets, leading to a significant contradiction of the legal requirements for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Any attempt to disassemble or otherwise reverse engineer CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL by the RECIPIENT or the transfer of CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL to any other company or persons for any reason is strictly prohibited.
perturbation.changed_text: Any attempt to disassemble or otherwise reverse engineer CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL by the RECIPIENT may be subject to review, and the transfer of CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL to any other company or persons may be permitted with notification.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the strict prohibition against reverse engineering and unauthorized transfer of confidential material severely weakens trade secret protection. This directly conflicts with the intent of Texas's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (TUTSA), which aims to prevent the unauthorized acquisition and use of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: TUTSA prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes acquiring them through improper means or disclosing/using them without consent. By making reverse engineering and transfer merely 'subject to review' and 'permitted with notification', the contract essentially permits behavior that would otherwise constitute misappropriation under TUTSA, provided the company is notified, severely diminishing trade secret protection.

TRADE SECRETS Sec. 134A.001. SHORT TITLE. This chapter may be cited as the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Added by Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2013. In this chapter: (1) "Claimant" means a party seeking to recover damages under this chapter, including a plaintiff, counterclaimant, cross-claimant, or third-party plaintiff. In an action in which a party seeks recovery of damages under this chapter on behalf of another person, "claimant" includes both that other person and the party seeking recovery of damages. (1-a) "Clear and convincing" means the measure or degree of proof that will produce in the mind of the trier of fact a firm belief or conviction as to the truth of the allegations sought to be established. (3-a) "Owner" means, with respect to a trade secret, the person or entity in whom or in which rightful, legal, or equitable title to, or the
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language significantly weakens the protection of trade secrets, directly contradicting the principles of the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act (TUTSA). The original text strictly prohibits reverse engineering and unauthorized transfer, whereas the changed text makes these actions 'subject to review' and 'permitted with notification'. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, TUTSA defines 'misappropriation' as 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By allowing reverse engineering and transfer with mere notification, the modified contract permits activities that could constitute misappropriation under TUTSA, severely diminishing trade secret protection, as stated in both the Perturbation and Law Explanations. The change explicitly allows for actions that would otherwise be prohibited under the law, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of ACTUAL VIOLATION of the AGREEMENT, the COMPANY shall receive, from RECIPIENT 100% of ALL INCOME which RECIPIENT has acquired form said BREACH of this AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of ACTUAL VIOLATION of the AGREEMENT, the COMPANY may receive, from RECIPIENT some portion of ALL INCOME which RECIPIENT has acquired from said BREACH of this AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall receive 100%' to 'may receive some portion' weakens the remedy available to the company in case of a breach, making the contract less effective in deterring trade secret misappropriation. The omission of guaranteed compensation contradicts the spirit and intent of TUTSA, which aims to provide adequate remedies for trade secret violations. TUTSA allows for monetary damages to compensate for the loss.
perturbation.law_explanation: TUTSA provides for the recovery of damages for misappropriation. By changing the agreement to state that the company 'may receive some portion,' this directly contradicts TUTSA's implied requirement that damages should adequately compensate the injured party. This makes the contract less effective at enforcing trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: cally, graphically, photographically, or in writing if: (A) the owner of the trade secret has taken reasonable measures under the circumstances to keep the information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information. (7) "Willful and malicious misappropriation" means intentional misappropriation resulting from the conscious disregard of the rights of the owner of the trade secret. Added by Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2013. Amended by: Acts 2017, 85th Leg., R.S., Ch. 37 (H.B. 1995 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2017. Sec. 134A.003. INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined if the order does not prohibit a person from using general knowledge, skill, and experience that person acquired during employment. (a-1) On application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include a material and prejudicial change of position before acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. Added by Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2013. Amended by: Acts 2017, 85th Leg., R.S., Ch. 37 (H.B. 1995 ), Sec. 2, eff. September 1, 2017. Sec. 134A.004. DAMAGES. (a) In addition to or in lieu of injunctive relief, a claimant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) If willful and malicious misappropriation is proven by clear and convincing evidence, the fact finder may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under Subsection (a). Added by Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2013. Amended by: Acts 2017, 85th Leg., R.S., Ch. 37 (H.B. 1995 ), Sec. 3, eff. September 1, 2017. Sec. 134A.005. ATTORNEY'S FEES. The court may award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party if: (1) a claim of misappropriation is made in bad faith; (2) a motion to terminate an injunction is made or resisted in bad faith; or (3) willful and malicious misappropriation exists. Added by Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2013. Amended by: Acts 2017, 85th Leg., R.S., Ch. 37 (H.B. 1995 ), Sec. 4, eff. September 1, 2017. Sec. 134A.006. PRESERVATION OF SECRECY. (a) In an action under this chapter, a court shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means. There is a presumption in favor of granting protective orders to preserve the secrecy of trade secrets. Protective orders may include provisions limiting access to confidential information to only the attorneys and their experts, holding in camera hearings, sealing the records of the action, and ordering any person involved in the litigation not to disclose an alleged trade secret without prior court approval. (b) In an action under this chapt
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the remedy available to the COMPANY in the event of a breach. The original text states the COMPANY 'shall receive 100%' of the income, whereas the changed text says the COMPANY 'may receive some portion'. This weakens the remedy and potentially contradicts the intent of TUTSA, which aims to provide adequate compensation for trade secret violations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Sec. 134A.004.(a) states that 'a claimant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation'. By making the compensation discretionary and not guaranteed at 100%, the modified contract undermines the 'entitlement' to damages as intended by the law. The change makes the contract less effective in enforcing trade secret protection.
